# The Bridge-Keeper's Ledger

*source text — do not edit after this visit*

I keep the bridge the way my mother kept her kettle: on, always, whether
or not anyone is coming for tea. Six years now. The river below has three
moods and I have learned all three, though I could not tell you which
one is honest. In flood it says nothing and takes what it wants. In
drought it complains, all afternoon, about a debt somebody owes it. In
the third mood — the one I don't have a name for, the one it wears maybe
twenty days a year — it just moves, quiet, like it's trying not to wake
the fish.

My post is the middle span, where the old stones give way to the new
ones. You can see the seam if you know to look. I was taught to read the
weather in the seam: damp means rain by evening, dry-and-cold means the
wind is turning to come from my sister's village instead of the sea.
I think this is true. I have never checked. Some of what I know I
witnessed with my own eyes; some of it I was told by the keeper before
me, an old man who liked to test whether I'd swallow anything; and some
of it I must have dreamed, because I cannot remember learning it and yet
there it is, sure as the stones.

Twice a year someone asks me to promise the bridge will hold. I always
say yes. I have never once been certain, and I have never once been
wrong, and I no longer think these two facts are related.

Tonight the seam is dry and cold. I put my mother's kettle on anyway. If
my sister's wind is coming, it will want something warm to arrive to,
whether or not it knows I am the one who put it there.
